data.title: Invesco Equity And Income Fund Q4 2025 Portfolio Positioning And Performance
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Invesco Equity And Income Fund Q4 2025 Commentary.

New holdings:

Becton Dickinson (BDX): Becton Dickinson develops and sells medical supplies and diagnostic products globally. Despite recent challenges from weaker pricing in China, slower diagnostics demand and funding cuts, the company has been spinning off its non-core Bioscience/Diagnostic segment, which we believe should improve growth rates in the remaining business. Longer term, we believe Becton is at the beginning of a portfolio transition that will address depressed sales growth, margin improvement and capital returns.

Ashtead (ASHTF): Operating as Sunbelt Rentals in North America, Ashtead rents construction and industrial equipment. While rental revenue hit record highs, profitability has slipped due to lower utilization and a soft construction market. The company's "Sunbelt 4.0" initiative aims to improve utilization, profit margins and growth. Ashtead has also been moving its primary listing to the New York Stock Exchange, which we believe will lift its valuation.

Schlumberger (SLB): As the world's largest oilfield services provider, Schlumberger has lagged the energy sector and has been trading near historic lows. Its business mix now appears to favor higher margin segments, supported by rising rig counts, new contracts and acquisition synergies. In our view, Schlumberger's scale and global reach position it well for improved profitability and growth.

Qnity Electronics (Q): The stock was added to the fund as the result of a spin off from DuPont (DD) in early November. Qnity is a global leader in advanced electronics materials, serving the semiconductor and AI infrastructure markets. We see AI acceleration and improvement in other end markets as a catalyst for stable, long-term growth.

Eliminated Holdings:

Fiserv (FI): Shares of Fiserv dropped sharply after management reported disappointing third quarter results and lowered full year guidance. The company launched the "One Fiserv" initiative and made leadership changes in an effort to address these issues, but ongoing uncertainty and a shift in our investment thesis led us to sell the position.

Pfizer (PFE): Purchased in 2023 amid pandemic-related declines, Pfizer faced weak Covid franchise sales and patent expirations. In our view, overspending on acquisitions and a weak internal product pipeline limited growth prospects. We sold to reallocate capital to stocks with better risk-reward opportunities.

T-Mobile US, Inc. -14.76 -0.14

From an equity sector standpoint, stock selection in IT, communication services and consumer staples detracted from relative return. The fund's holdings in high grade fixed income and convertible securities also detracted from relative return. Stock selection in industrials, consumer discretionary and health aided relative return.

Contributors to performance (equity only)

Coherent: Earnings beat expectations and shares rose due to surging AI data center demand, strong growth in networking revenue and expanding profit margins supported by a more focused, high-return product portfolio.

Alphabet: Shares of Google's parent company rose due to strong growth in its Gemini AI platform. The company also reported strong advertising and cloud revenues. A favorable outcome in the company's antitrust case also sent shares higher.

Wells Fargo: The bank delivered strong earnings results driven by higher revenue, improved credit quality and higher investment banking fees. Wells Fargo also raised its dividend and repurchased shares, now permissible following the Fed's removal of its asset cap.

Merck: The drugmaker reported earnings and revenues that beat expectations, driven by strong sales of its cancer treatment Keytruda and its pulmonary arterial hypertension therapy WinReva. Merck also announced the acquisition of Verona Pharma (VRNA), which is expected to boost its product pipeline.

Detractors from performance (equity only)

Fiserv: Shares declined after the company reported disappointing third quarter results.

Revenue and earnings fell short of expectations, prompting management to slash its full year earnings forecast and cut guidance for organic growth. We sold this position during the quarter.

Oracle: Shares of the stock tumbled following its earnings release, which was weaker than expected, particularly regarding revenues. Oracle's $15 billion AI infrastructure investment and debt issuance also appeared to raise investor concerns about leverage and near-term profitability.

Sysco: The food service distributor reported earnings and revenue that were above expectations. However, food service volumes declined amid weak restaurant demand and ongoing inflation pressures.

Microsoft: Despite strong earnings overall, shares declined due to concerns about heavy AI infrastructure spending, which appeared to raise questions about near-term profit margins. Broader market volatility and valuation pressures on big technology stocks amplified the stock's pullback.

About Risk

Active trading results in added expenses and may result in a lower return and increased tax liability.

Convertible securities may be affected by market interest rates, the risk of issuer default, the value of the underlying stock, or the issuer's right to buy back the convertible securities.

An issuer may be unable to meet interest and/or principal payments, thereby causing its instruments to decrease in value and lowering the issuer's credit rating.

Derivatives may be more volatile and less liquid than traditional investments and are subject to market, interest rate, credit, leverage, counterparty, and management risks. An investment in a derivative could lose more than the cash amount invested.

In general, stock values fluctuate, sometimes widely, in response to activities specific to the company as well as general market, economic and political conditions.

The risks of investing in securities of foreign issuers can include fluctuations in foreign currencies, political and economic instability, and foreign taxation issues.

Interest rate risk refers to the risk that bond prices generally fall as interest rates rise and vice versa.

The investment techniques and risk analysis used by the portfolio managers may not produce the desired results.

The value, interest rates, and liquidity of non-cash paying instruments, such as zero coupon and pay-in-kind securities, are subject to greater fluctuation than other types of securities.

Preferred securities may include provisions that permit the issuer to defer or omit distributions for a certain period of time, and reporting the distribution for tax purposes may be required, even though the income may not have been received. Further, preferred securities may lose substantial value due to the omission or deferment of dividend payments.

Stocks of small and medium-sized companies tend to be more vulnerable to adverse developments, may be more volatile, and may be illiquid or restricted as to resale.

Obligations issued by US Government agencies and instrumentalities may receive varying levels of support from the government, which could affect the fund's ability to recover should they default.

Sharpe Ratio is a risk-adjusted measure calculated using standard deviation and excess return to determine reward per unit of risk. A higher Sharpe ratio indicates better risk-adjusted performance. Information Ratio is a measure of portfolio returns beyond the returns of a benchmark, usually an index, compared to the volatility of those returns. Standard deviation measures a fund's range of total returns and identifies the spread of a fund's short-term fluctuations. Tracking Error is defined as the expected standard deviation of a portfolio's excess return over the benchmark index return. The up and down capture measures how well a manager was able to replicate or improve on periods of positive benchmark returns and how severely the manager was affected by periods of negative benchmark returns. Maximum Drawdown is the maximum observed loss from a high to a low of a portfolio, before a new high is attained. Maximum drawdown is an indicator of downside risk over a specified time period. Weighted Average Market Cap is a measure of the average size of company held in a portfolio. The percentage of the portfolio invested each company, or its weight, is multiplied by its size (market capitalization). An average of the weighted size of all companies held is then calculated. Price/earnings measures the price per share relative to the earnings per share of the company while excluding extraordinary items. Price to book measures the firm's capitalization (market price) to book value. Est. 3-5 year EPS (Earning per share) growth measures the earning per share growth from FY3 to FY5. ROE is the Return on Equity that measures the fund's annual return relative to total shareholders' equity. This ratio evaluates how quickly investments can be turned into profits. Long-term debt to capital measures a fund's financial leverage by calculating the proportion of long-term debt used to finance its assets relative to the amount of equity used for the same purpose. A higher ratio indicates higher leverage. Operating margin measures the profit a fund makes for every dollar of sales after paying the variable expenses. Contribution to Return measures the performance impact from portfolio holdings over a defined time period. It takes into account both weight and performance of the portfolio holdings. Contribution to Return is calculated at security level.

data.title: Invesco Equity And Income Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key takeaways

1 The fund underperformed its benchmark

Stock selection in information technology (IT), communication services and consumer staples contributed to underperformance. Conversely, selection in industrials, consumer discretionary and health care aided the fund's relative return.

2 Fixed income and convertible security holdings detracted from relative return

The fund holds US Treasuries and high-grade corporate bonds and convertible securities as an income source and to help provide a measure of stability in volatile markets. These holdings lagged the Russell 1000 Value Index.

3 The team added several new holdings amid volatility during the quarter

We purchased several new fund holdings across various sectors. We also eliminated several holdings, using the proceeds to fund investments we believe have a better risk/reward profile.

Manager perspective and outlook

  • US equity markets experienced volatility in the fourth quarter amid economic crosscurrents and seemingly mixed investor sentiment toward artificial intelligence (AI) valuations and AI-related spending.
  • The longest government shutdown in US history appeared to further dampen confidence, as key economic data were delayed. Once released, the initial third-quarter Us Gdp estimate showed robust growth of 4.3%; however, labor market conditions softened. Inflation moderated but stayed above the US Federal Reserve's (Fed) 2% target.
  • The Fed cut interest rates twice during the quarter, lowering the target range to 3.50%–3.75% in an effort to support growth amid labor market weakness and easing inflation pressures.
  • Despite a volatility spike mid-quarter, strong corporate earnings and Fed rate cuts fueled equity gains. The S&P 500 Index (SP500) returned 2.66% for the quarter, with improved market breadth.
  • High valuations, geopolitical tensions, labor market weakness and elevated tariff rates have remained concerns going into 2026, which could prompt additional volatility.
  • Regardless of the economic environment, we continue to focus on our fundamental analysis so we can move quickly to take advantage of new opportunities as they become available.
  • As always, we seek to invest in companies with attractive valuations and strong fundamentals, qualities we believe will ultimately be reflected in those companies' stock prices.

data.title: Janus Henderson Enterprise Fund Q4 2025 Portfolio Review
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Janus Henderson Enterprise Fund Q4 2025 Commentary.

We were pleased to see many of our disciplined growth-oriented investments rewarded during the quarter.

Relative contributors included J.B. Hunt Transport Services (JBHT), a provider of trucking and intermodal freight solutions. Shares of J.B. Hunt rose as investors started to see signs of a recovery in freight shipment volumes, following several years of a cyclical slowdown. The company's management team expects pricing to improve rapidly as shipping demand accelerates, given supply constraints and driver shortages. This could have a positive impact on margins and earnings growth. We also have welcomed the company's disciplined approach to capacity investments and cost-cutting, which could further boost profitability.

Revolution Medicines (RVMD), another contributor, is a clinical-stage precision oncology company developing advanced cancer therapies. The stock rose on encouraging early-stage clinical trial results for RMC-9805. This novel therapy is being investigated as a potential treatment that extends and improves the quality of life for people diagnosed with pancreatic cancer. The company also hopes that the innovation behind this treatment may have applications in treating other cancers, which could grow the size of its addressable market.

Constellation Software (CSU:CA) was a relative detractor. Constellation has established a successful track record of acquiring and improving the operations of "mission-critical" software businesses that target industry verticals, i.e., market segments with a high degree of customer retention and relatively low competitive intensity. Constellation's visionary founder and CEO is retiring due to health concerns, and uncertainty around this transition weighed on the stock price. Additionally, enterprise software companies more broadly have been pressured by concerns that AI could act as a competitive disrupter, lowering barriers to entry, enabling new competition, or upending existing business models. We believe Constellation would be relatively insulated from such disruption due to its strong customer relationships and highly specific industry expertise, which would be difficult for other companies to replicate. We remain invested in the stock.

CoStar Group (CSGP), another detractor, provides real estate information and analytics to the residential and commercial property markets. It also owns Apartments.com, a leading marketplace for finding apartment rentals, and Homes.com, which provides listings of homes for sale and rent. Despite the company's solid financial results and guidance, the stock sold off on competitive concerns after Google (GOOGL) started testing the placement of real estate listings in Google Search. Investors also worried that consumers may start to use AI chatbots to search for rentals. We believe these competitive concerns are overstated. The power of AI depends on the quality of the information inputs, and CoStar benefits from tremendous information advantages and proprietary data that would be difficult to replicate. The company also has continued to invest in technological innovations to build its competitive positioning and improve the user experience.

At a glance

Performance

The Fund returned 1.75% and the Russell Midcap® Growth Index (RMCCG) returned -3.70%.

Contributors/detractors

Stock selection in the healthcare and industrials sectors contributed to relative performance. Holdings in the consumer discretionary and real estate sectors detracted, due in part to stock selection.

Outlook

We remain committed to a disciplined, bottom-up investment approach that pursues growth opportunities while seeking to manage downside risk.

Investment environment

  • Stocks rose in the fourth quarter on positive corporate earnings news and hopes for Federal Reserve (Fed) rate cuts. The Fed followed up a September rate cut with two 25 basis-point (BP) rate cuts, in October and December, respectively.
  • Economic growth remains resilient despite slower-than-expected job growth. Inflation also has been relatively subdued, creating breathing room for the Fed potentially to cut rates further.
  • The quarter saw some recalibration of the exuberance around artificial intelligence (AI). As a result, we saw a broadening of the market and renewed focus on fundamentals, which benefited our fundamentals-driven investment approach.

Portfolio review

We were pleased to see many of our disciplined growth-oriented investments rewarded during the quarter. Relative contributors included J.B. Hunt Transport Services (JBHT), a provider of trucking and intermodal freight solutions. Shares of J.B. Hunt rose as investors started to see signs of a recovery in freight shipment volumes, following several years of a cyclical slowdown. The company also has continued to invest in technological innovations to build its competitive positioning and improve the user experience.

Manager outlook

We remain constructive on the market outlook for 2026. Consumer spending appears resilient despite slower job growth, and moderating inflation has given the Fed more flexibility to cut interest rates. While the extent of Fed easing will depend on the interplay of economic and inflation data, we believe lower interest rates and potential fiscal stimulus

heading into 2026 midterm elections could provide an economic tailwind that benefits a broad range of companies. In the mid-cap market, we welcome a renewed focus on profitability and fundamentals. However, we remain concerned about some areas of the market where valuations appear extended. We also recognize we could continue to see shifting investor sentiment around new technologies such as AI. While we believe the multi-year investment trajectory for AI remains constructive, we caution that we could see periods of volatility in AI-related stocks as investors try to assess how quickly or powerfully these investments might be rewarded. Additionally, while AI may spur tremendous productivity gains and long-term economic benefits, its impact on job growth and consumer spending will be an important trend to watch in 2026. For our part, we remain on the lookout for opportunities to invest in AI, but only with companies that meet our investment criteria of sustainable earnings growth, durable competitive advantages, strong management teams, and above-average earnings potential. We also remain on the lookout for opportunities to capitalize on other exciting secular growth trends including healthcare innovation, electrification, and reshoring. We believe this diversified, risk-aware approach may help us perform in a variety of market environments while also managing downside risk.

data.title: Whale's Tracking - Reassessment
data.description:
From Market Swings to Governance Risk

A more widely shared view is emerging: what many describe as “loss of control” is not an emotional reaction to a single headline, but a rational repricing of institutional boundaries being repeatedly tested - and possibly crossed.

The criminal investigation involving Powell goes to the heart of modern finance: central bank independence. If monetary policymakers can face legal pressure for their decisions, markets must include “governance risk” directly in discount rates.

In that context, periods of strength in some traditional haven currencies are not just about better fundamentals; they increasingly look like defensive positioning as uncertainty around the US system rises.

At the same time, tariff actions linked to the Greenland dispute underline that trade policy is shifting from an economic tool to a geopolitical one. When tariffs can quickly move from rivals to allies, and when the trigger is less about industrial goals or the trade balance and more about political issues, then corporate profits, supply-chain costs, and cross-border capital flows become harder to forecast.

For institutions, the implication is straightforward: almost anything can be turned into a policy tool. Tariffs can reshape cost structures; the dollar can be used as a tool of financial pressure; and equity markets can be treated as a scoreboard for political success. Macro data still matters, but in this environment, it has less influence on risk appetite and policy expectations.

For years, global allocation rested on an assumption of US institutional stability and policy continuity. Even when tensions rose, markets tended to assume things would return to a familiar track. But when governance conflict shifts from rhetoric to action - investigations, sanctions, and tariffs - this assumption becomes harder to hold. Risk premia then rise across a wider set of assets.

From an asset-pricing perspective, investors are adding a larger “governance uncertainty” component to standard models. That leads to a market that can look contradictory but, from an institutional point of view, is rational: indices may not fall immediately because earnings momentum and buybacks still support prices, but new money becomes less willing to buy at old valuations. Allocation behaviour shifts towards lower leverage, lower exposure, and lower correlation.

Importantly, this adjustment does not require a crash. For most institutional portfolios, risk management is gradual rather than all-or-nothing. A slow reduction in USD exposure is often expressed through less reinvestment rather than outright selling: new allocations are reduced, maturing positions are not rolled in full, hedge ratios rise, and part of the risk budget moves towards non-USD settlement channels or markets less tied to US policy. The result is that the dollar system’s marginal funding conditions can become more fragile in sentiment terms and more prone to sudden “liquidity discounts” during event shocks.

More Rallies, Less Follow-Through

In this macro regime, crypto markets look less like independent safe havens and more like a function of liquidity and risk appetite. This week’s price recovery is not unusual; in fact, when uncertainty rises, short-lived rebounds often become more common.

Short covering, a quick normalisation in futures basis, and short-term changes in stablecoin supply can all create upside. Yet institutional traders’ expectations have not meaningfully improved after this week’s rise. The basic reason is clear: when uncertainty around US fiscal and monetary policy increases, crypto struggles to attract steady, reliable liquidity.

Source: Amberdata Derivatives

Source: Amberdata Derivatives

This is easy to misunderstand. Intuitively, greater uncertainty about institutions should support “non-sovereign” assets. But market structure keeps crypto closely tied to the dollar system. Leverage, settlement, and key risk tools - especially derivatives and stablecoins - remain heavily USD-linked.

When dollar funding becomes harder to read, and when shocks are driven more by politics than by data, market-makers and institutions become more cautious with risk. Leverage shrinks faster, and liquidity becomes shorter-term, thinner, and more expensive. Crypto prices can still rise, but it becomes harder for rallies to turn into trends, because trends need stable, sustained, and affordable new buying.

There is another practical constraint. When macro noise rises, correlations across risk assets often increase in the short run. Crypto, as a higher-volatility instrument, is more likely to be used to adjust overall risk - meaning it may be reduced or hedged earlier than other exposures.

The pattern is familiar: rallies are driven by technical factors and short-term flows, while drawdowns are driven by tighter risk budgets and hedging-related rebalancing. For systematic and institutional capital, this is not necessarily a long-term rejection of crypto; it is a recognition that, when USD uncertainty rises, it is difficult to justify a higher “liquidity premium” for crypto.

Source: Amberdata Derivatives

A more profound change is that inflation and employment - once central to the market’s policy framework - are being pushed aside by political priorities. Markets used to link expectations closely to data, creating a relatively stable reaction function: higher inflation lifted rate expectations, weaker employment increased the chance of easing, and asset prices moved around that logic.

But when political issues can directly drive tariffs, investigations, sanctions, or regulatory direction, the reaction function becomes less consistent. The informational value of data falls, and event risk becomes the main driver. Investors are therefore spending more time assessing whether policy paths are workable and where institutional limits really are, rather than simply trading the next data release.

This also weakens a stabiliser that markets once relied on. The “central bank put” is the idea that, in severe stress, monetary authorities will step in to prevent financial conditions from tightening too sharply.

But when central bank independence is challenged, and central bankers are drawn into political conflict, the credibility of that backstop is reduced. Institutions respond in predictable ways: heavier hedging, shorter duration, lower concentration in one currency system, and stronger diversification across assets, regions, and legal frameworks.

To be clear, institutions have not responded to recent US policy and institutional shocks with panic selling. But they are reducing reliance on USD-linked exposure in a slower, more systematic way - one that is harder to see in headline moves.

For USD assets, this means valuations depend more on changes in governance-related risk premia, and risk budgets are more easily triggered by events. For crypto, it means short-term rebounds may happen more often, but sustained trend rallies are harder to maintain without a stable liquidity backdrop.

Politics is shifting markets from a data-driven to an event-driven regime. The institutional response is rarely about betting on a single outcome; it is about updating constraints in advance: reducing single-system exposure, keeping liquidity, strengthening hedges, and waiting for a new pricing anchor to emerge.

data.title: Whale's Tradingview: How To Trade In 2026? (Part 2)
data.description:
What most warrants reappraisal in 2026 is not the linear question of whether growth will be stronger, but the fact that markets are increasingly working with a different pricing grammar. For roughly two decades, asset returns have tended to rest on two tacit assumptions.

First, supply chains were organised to maximise efficiency, keeping costs down and inflation contained. Secondly, central banks provided decisive backstops during stress, systematically compressing risk premia. Both assumptions are now weakening.

Supply chains increasingly prioritise control and redundancy, fiscal and industrial policy more frequently feeds directly into earnings expectations, and geopolitics has shifted from a tail risk to a persistent source of noise. In that sense, “regionalisation” is less a slogan than a change in the constraints under which the economic system operates.

Within this framework, the central task is not to bet on a single macro outcome, but to realign exposures towards three more observable “hard variables”: supply constraints, capital expenditure, and policy-backed order flow.

Together, these variables point towards a familiar cluster of assets - commodity-linked equities, the AI infrastructure stack, defence and security themes, and selected non-US markets that can improve a portfolio’s correlation structure.

Meanwhile, the core question in rates and sovereign bonds is no longer how much of a tailwind a “rate cut narrative” can deliver, but how a reshaped term structure redistributes returns.

Regionalisation: Not “Decoupling,” but a New Cost Function

Treating regionalisation as synonymous with full-scale decoupling tends to understate its practical significance. A more accurate description is that the objective function of globalisation has shifted from “efficiency at all costs” to “efficiency subject to security constraints”.

Once security becomes binding, variables that previously sat outside most valuation models, such as supply chain redundancy, energy security, access to critical minerals, export controls on strategic technologies, and the rigidity of defence budgets, begin to show up in discount rates and earnings assumptions in tangible ways.

Two immediate consequences follow for asset pricing. First, risk premia become less likely to revert to structurally low levels: political and policy uncertainty turns into a day-to-day input, and markets demand compensation for bearing it. Few investors willingly carry “Cuban equity risk”; in the current regime, even US equities can no longer assume that risk is priced at zero.

Secondly, global beta explains less, while regional alpha matters more. Under different geopolitical blocs and policy functions, the same growth and inflation prints can translate into materially different valuations and capital flows. For allocators, diversification in a regionalised world increasingly means diversifying by supply chain position and policy sensitivity - not simply spreading exposure across countries.

Equities: From “Buying Growth” to “Buying Position”

If equity allocation between 2010 and 2021 essentially amounted to “buying growth alongside falling discount rates,” 2026 looks closer to “buying position.” Position, in this context, is a market’s location on three maps: the resource map, the compute map, and the security map. As supply chain autonomy and critical infrastructure resilience move up the agenda, markets at key nodes can command structural premia - even when domestic macro conditions are less than ideal.

In a world where security becomes a first-order priority, increasing inventories of gold, silver, copper, and other industrial metals can be rational even without immediate end-use demand. Supply chains can be disrupted abruptly - trade tensions have already shown how quickly frictions can turn into cost shocks.

That reality pushes major economies towards larger strategic mineral buffers. Structural uplift in critical minerals demand, combined with long-cycle supply constraints, makes commodities behave more like supply-side assets than mere mirrors of the business cycle.

This logic strengthens the case for allocating to resource-endowed equity markets. Copper-linked equities in countries such as Chile partly reflect the foundational demand created by electrification and the build-out of industrial infrastructure.

Precious metals and broader resource equities in markets such as South Africa, meanwhile, carry a double-edged profile: on the way up, earnings and FX can reinforce one another; in risk-off phases, politics and external financing conditions can magnify volatility. Resource country equities, therefore, fit better as “supply-constraint factors” within a portfolio than as generic emerging-market beta.

A second central theme is AI. Application-layer narratives easily capture discussion, but capital allocation is often better anchored in what sits plainly on balance sheets: compute, power, data centres, networking and cooling. The common feature across these segments is greater capex visibility, frequently reinforced by both policy and industrial strategy.

Rather than treating AI as a software valuation cycle, it can be framed as an infrastructure build-out: rising compute density ultimately translates into demand for electricity and engineering, shifting a larger share of returns towards upstream and midstream physical capacity.

Under regionalisation, higher requirements for redundancy and localisation raise the strategic value of critical hardware and intermediate inputs. Markets such as South Korea - home to key semiconductors and electronics linkages - often provide a more direct equity expression of the AI capex cycle, precisely because they sit at the industrial interface of global compute infrastructure. From a portfolio perspective, the appeal lies not only in higher growth potential but also in more visible capex and more durable policy support.

Defence and security have likewise returned to the forefront of investor attention for the first time since the end of the Cold War. Influenced by a more transactional US foreign policy posture and the Russia–Ukraine war, both the United States and Europe have elevated defence and security as strategic priorities.

The defining feature of defence-linked assets is that demand is not driven by marginal household consumption, but by a fiscal function constrained by national security.

Once budgets step up, political resistance to reversal tends to be strong, improving order visibility. That gives defence and security equities a more defensive role in a regionalised regime: as conflict and sanctions risk rise, they can add resilience at the portfolio level.

Still, defence and security often reprice ahead of fundamentals. Rapid, event-driven reratings followed by mean reversion are common. A more robust positioning is to treat the theme as tail-risk insurance or a risk-hedging factor, rather than as a straightforward growth compounder. Its value is in limiting drawdowns, not in delivering consistent outperformance every quarter.

Hong Kong SAR and China assets also merit consideration. Describing them simply as “cheap” is incomplete; their allocation value typically comes from two sources.

First, risk pricing often embeds pessimism early, creating rebalancing optionality. Secondly, their policy functions and sector composition differ from those of the those of US and European markets, which can improve the correlation structure.

In a regionalised world, correlations do not automatically fall; they can rise during stress, making structurally differentiated exposures more useful as hedges.

Rates & Treasuries: Staying Steep in a Term-Premium Regime

The core tension in 2026 rates can be summarised simply: the front end largely responds to the policy path, while the long end behaves more like a term premium vessel.

Rate cut expectations can pull short yields lower, but whether the long end follows depends on whether inflation tail risks, fiscal supply pressure and political uncertainty allow term premia to compress further. Put differently, long-end “stubbornness” does not necessarily mean markets have miscounted the number of cuts; it may simply reflect markets charging again for long-horizon risk.

Compared to the same period last year, the yield of T-bills has declined significantly, but the decline has become smaller as the duration increases, while the yield of T-bonds has hardly dropped. Source: ustreasuryyieldcurve.com

Supply dynamics reinforce this structural divergence. Shifts in US Treasury financing strategy directly affect supply–demand balance across maturities: the front end is often absorbed more readily when money-market capacity is strong, whereas the long end is more prone to impulse moves driven by risk budgets and term-premium repricing.

The portfolio implication is straightforward: duration is best managed in layers, avoiding a single-path bet that “inflation disappears and term premia return to extreme lows.” Curve structure trades - steepeners in particular - persist not because they are clever, but because they align with the different pricing mechanisms at the front and back ends of the curve.

Crypto: Ring-fencing “Digital Commodity” Exposure from Second-Tier Risk

The key issue for crypto in 2026 is not whether the complex rises, but how sharply internal differentiation increases. Bitcoin is more readily framed as a non-sovereign asset with rules-based supply and cross-border portability - hence its tendency to attract alternative-asset and hedging demand under a regionalisation narrative.

By contrast, a number of tokens sit closer to equity-like risk assets: their pricing depends more heavily on growth stories, ecosystem expansion and risk appetite. When risk-free rates remain attractive, regulatory frameworks become clearer, and traditional capital markets offer more mature routes for funding and exit, equity-like tokens need to offer meaningfully higher risk compensation to remain compelling from an allocation standpoint.

Clearly, altcoins have struggled to attract investor interest over the past year, and their market share has declined further. Source: TradingView

For that reason, crypto exposure is better managed through ring-fencing than through a single basket. Bitcoin fits more naturally within a commodity/alternatives sleeve, where small weights can buy asymmetry at the portfolio level.

Equity-like tokens are better treated as high-volatility risk assets, subject to stricter return hurdles and explicit risk budgets. In a regionalised regime, the aim is not to embrace every new instrument, but to identify which exposures remain intelligible under the new constraints.

Anchor on Hard Constraints, Monetise Structural Divergence

Taken together, the 2026 portfolio increasingly resembles the management of hard constraints. Supply constraints restore the strategic character of commodities and resource equities. Capex visibility supports the AI infrastructure stack.

Policy-backed order flow underpins the resilience of defence and security. Term-premium repricing reshapes duration’s return distribution. Selected non-US exposures can provide reflexive hedging through valuation structure and policy-function differences.

None of this requires perfect prediction of each headline. The scarcer skill in a regionalised era is building portfolios that rely less on flawless forecasting: hard assets and infrastructure absorb structural demand, curve structure expresses structural divergence, and hedging factors absorb structural noise. Trading in 2026 is less about guessing the answer and more about recognising the constraints, then reshaping allocation priorities accordingly.

Disclaimer: The information provided herein does not constitute investment advice, financial advice, trading advice, or any other sort of advice, and should not be treated as such. All content set out below is for informational purposes only.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Quantum: Turnaround Is On Track But Shares Aren't Yet A Buy
data.description:
I've been a skeptic of a number of quantum-themed stocks over the past year.

That would include Quantum Corporation (QMCO), which I wrote about with a bearish perspective in September, arguing that it was not a good play for either AI or quantum computing. The stock has lost more than a third of its value since then, so that turned out to be directionally on target.

However, the company's FY'26 Q2 results were surprisingly upbeat, leading me to upgrade QMCO stock to a Hold in November. And this past week, we saw further signs of improvement at Quantum Corp. Interestingly, however, QMCO stock hasn't really started to rebound yet:

I believe QMCO stock soared in early 2025 based around enthusiasm from other quantum computing stocks, such as IonQ (IONQ) and Rigetti Computing (RGTI), rather than positive developments with Quantum Corp. in particular. In fact, as I detailed in my first article on the company, this company doesn't have much at all to do with quantum computing, and as such, there wasn't any underlying fundamental reason why it should be rallying in sympathy with IonQ or Rigetti.

Now, though, Quantum Corp.'s fundamentals are starting to improve, while the valuation has come back to earth. So, at this point, is QMCO stock finally moving toward being a buying opportunity?

Quantum's Upbeat Q3 Guidance Update

The most interesting fundamental bit of news around the company came last week when the company pre-announced its Q3 revenues. In the release, the company stated that it generated approximately $72.7 million in revenues for the quarter.

This $72.7 million comes as a pleasant surprise. The Street was expecting $66.4 million for the upcoming quarter. And the company itself had previously guided to a range of between $65 million and $69 million. So the actual results are coming in well above the high end of Quantum Corp.'s prior expectations.

To give this revenue figure more context, consider this longer-term chart of the company's trailing twelve month revenues over the past decade:

Revenues have been steadily declining since 2023, and more broadly have been in a downtrend since 2017.

Particularly with the sharp drop in 2024, this appeared to be a company that was potentially in terminal decline. In a world of cloud solutions, wasn't tape storage an increasingly obsolete technology, at least for mass market use cases?

And yet, with this Q3 result, it appears that Quantum Corp. is going to post a positive year-over-year revenue figure. It's not much; the company posted $72.6 million in the same period of the prior year, so it's a less than one percent increase on a year-over-year basis. But it is an increase, and that's a sharp contrast from the past two years of results. More encouragingly, Quantum Corp. posted just $62.7 million of revenues in Q2 of the current fiscal year, so this will be a double-digit revenue increase sequentially.

It remains to be seen what portion of this jump is cyclical or timing-based, as opposed to what part of this revenue increase is coming from new customers and organic growth. That said, if this positive trend change continues and the company can post something like $75 million in revenues in Q4, that'd start to give the bulls real evidence that the company has turned the corner and is on a sustainable recovery path.

One quarter alone doesn't fully mark a turnaround. Quantum Corp. was able to grow revenues in 2019 and in 2021-2022 despite the longer-term downward revenue trajectory. So a short-term reversal in the revenue slump isn't necessarily proof that the company is back on track for good. However, Quantum's new leadership team has shown a lot of initiative so far, and there's decent reason for optimism based on the most recent results.

I will be curious to see in the Q3 results where the revenue growth is coming from. From my perspective, it seems somewhat unlikely that Quantum Corp. is benefitting much from the surge in demand that we're seeing at memory companies like Sandisk (SNDK). The use case for AI/data center memory and storage is far different from the typical client that is interested in tape storage solutions. For example, a high-profile recent client win for Quantum was getting the Library of Congress to sign up for a new data preservation contract. That's a rather different task than AI/data center driven data storage.

However, if there is any tailwind, even a modest one, from the broader memory and storage push that is influencing Quantum's results, that could potentially be a game-changer in terms of reshaping investor sentiment around this company in the future.

And it's at least theoretically possible that some AI-related demand will seep into the tape storage side of the market. I'd highlight a comment from Quantum Corp. CEO Hugues Meyrath on the Q2 earnings conference call which could speak to how tape may be a component of broader data storage solutions in the age of AI:

"Last week [in November 2025], we introduced new capabilities in ActiveScale that fundamentally changed what cold data can do. With industry first ranged, restore and more than 5x faster access to small objects, customers can now pull back only the data they need instead of rehydrating entire files. That means long-term archive and AI data lakes can behave like active query ready data sets. Cold data becomes live data, instantly usable for AI training, inference, analytics, compliance, you name it. No other vendor can do that.

On the innovation front, I also want to highlight our strategic partnership with Entanglement. They chose Quantum as the storage fabric for their next-generation AI and HPC data centers. The future of AI isn't just about faster compute. It's about secure, scalable, high durability data infrastructure that can feed those compute engines continuously and efficiently. Our solutions give them exactly that: tiered sovereign cyber resilience storage with the scale and performance AI requires."

I'm still not convinced that AI is going to be all that meaningful of a driver for Quantum's results in coming years. This is still a company focused on tape storage, after all. But it's worth keeping an eye on developments here in case AI emerges as a more powerful demand generator than currently expected.

There are more tangible positive developments to point to as well. Quantum Corp. changed its distribution partner in the Asian market, leading to an immediate surge in revenues from that region. The company's backlog increased dramatically in Q2, which appears to already be turning into positive momentum with Q3's preannounced results. And Quantum Corp's gross margin rebounded a bit in Q2 as well, showing some potential progress on pricing power.

QMCO Stock Verdict

Quantum Corp. is in a bit of an enigmatic situation right now. The company's operating results appear to be showing the early signs of a potentially successful turnaround. Normally, that'd be something to get excited about.

However, much of the shareholder base of QMCO stock likely owns the company because they are excited about quantum computing. And, as such, the fact that stocks like IonQ have cooled off means that Quantum Corp. is struggling to rally even as it posts encouraging operating results.

Adding to that, due to the aforementioned quantum computing enthusiasm, QMCO stock was previously trading at a lofty valuation. After the tumble in Quantum Corp. shares, in combination with the improvement in operating results, I think you can reasonably argue that there is a case for a speculative buy here.

But the company is still facing some serious structural challenges and has much further to go before getting back to breakeven on a GAAP profitability basis. Indeed, the company lost $32 million in operations over the past 12 months. Throw in the $26 million paid in interest over that period on top of that and Quantum remains deeply in the red. A modest uptick in revenues won't fix the business model, there needs to be double-digit top-line growth for Quantum to have a reasonably quick route to profitability.

As such, while acknowledging the solid improvement in the firm's trajectory over the past couple of quarters, I'm sticking with a Hold rating for now.

data.title: Old National Bancorp Isn't Ready For An Upgrade Just Yet
data.description:
A little over a year ago, in early January of 2025, I made the painful decision to downgrade shares of Old National Bancorp (ONB) from a Buy to a Hold. This decision was not made lightly. And it was driven by a combination of factors, including the pricing of the stock and changes in asset quality. Having said that, even though I wasn't happy to downgrade the stock, I was happy with my overall rating history of it. Since I had previously rated the company a Buy, the stock had risen 51.4%, materially outperforming the 38.8% rise that the S&P 500 (SP500) saw. So even though it was disappointing to adopt a more neutral outlook, at least investors enjoyed the ride up while it lasted.

Fast forward to today, and the stock has unfortunately underperformed the market. The stock is up only 12.3% while the S&P 500 is up 17.3%. This is in spite of the fact that revenue, profits, and balance sheet data, have all come in pretty strong. Net interest margin is improving and asset quality is good but not great. If the stock was cheaper, I would probably use this opportunity to upgrade the company. But right now, I believe that maintaining it as a ‘hold’ makes the most sense. It is important to note that, on January 21st, management will be announcing financial results for the final quarter of the 2024 fiscal year. Leading up to that point, analysts are optimistic. But even if they turn out to be accurate, that's not enough to change my opinion just yet.

Too early to bank on Old National Bancorp again

Whenever I analyze a bank, I almost always like to start with the balance sheet, the most important aspect of which, in my opinion, would be deposits. Deposits are incredibly valuable because, even though they represent a liability, they serve as a single metric that helps to understand how fast the company is growing and can grow. During the most recent quarter, which was the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, deposits amounted to $55.01 billion. That represents an increase, a rather substantial one in fact, over the $40.82 billion that the company reported a year earlier.

Such rapid deposit growth is very uncommon with banks. But there is one case in which this deposit growth can occur. And that happens to be in the event of a merger or acquisition. Sure enough, on May 1st of last year, the management team at Old National Bancorp completed the firm's merger with Bremer Financial Corporation in a cash and stock deal valued at about $1.4 billion. This is not the only acquisition that the company has made over the last few years. Back in April of 2024, for instance, the company acquired CapStar Financial Holdings any deal that brought in about $2.56 billion of deposits. Clearly, this is a growth-oriented business and, as you will see shortly, that has proven to be successful.

It is worth mentioning that the company ended the most recent quarter with $3.37 billion of brokered deposits. That's approximately 6.1% of overall deposits. That's not really too high in my opinion. Typically, I am wary of brokered deposits because they are not guaranteed and because, since they come from other financial institutions, they cost more than traditional deposits due. For context, in the third quarter of 2025, these cost shareholders 4.40% annually. That compares to the 0.31% that savings accounts cost, the 1.53% that checking and NOW accounts cost, the 2.90% that money market accounts cost, and the 3.76% that non brokered time deposits cost. So I do typically prefer, all else being the same, brokered deposits being lower.

The value of loans also increased as deposits grew. They went from $35.89 billion at the end of 2024 to $47.40 billion in the third quarter of 2025. The greatest exposure that the company has here would be to commercial real estate. About $22.08 billion, or 46.6%, of the company's loan portfolio involves commercial real estate. Other commercial loans aren't far behind $14.51 billion. Residential real estate and consumer loans are much smaller by comparison. Whenever I see a lot of exposure to commercial real estate, I am always wary of exposure to office properties because of high and rising vacancy rates. The good news is that only about 5% of the entire loan portfolio for the company is tied up in these types of assets. That is relatively limited in the grand scheme of things and is lower than what most other banks that I look at have.

The value of securities has increased alongside other aspects of the balance sheet. The company ended the third quarter of last year with $14.82 billion worth on its books. This was up considerably the $10.79 billion that it had at the end of 2024. Cash and cash equivalents similarly grew, expanding from $1.23 billion to $1.68 billion. One thing that I am a bit wary about is the fact that debt continues to increase also. Given the cash component of the acquisition that I mentioned earlier, this was inevitable. Ultimately, debt grew from $5.41 billion to $6.77 billion. I wish that management would allocate some of the cash toward debt repayment. But at least the debt level was not so high that the bank seems to be at risk.

When it comes to the income statement, we have seen some pretty solid growth as of late. For the first nine months of the 2025 fiscal year, net interest income was $1.31 billion. That was up considerably from the $1.05 billion the institution reported a year earlier. Obviously, the increase in the size of the company's balance sheet played a role here. It also benefited, it's worth mentioning, from a rise in its net interest margin from 3.31% to 3.50%. This is worth some discussion. As interest rates drop, banks actually benefit. Deposits are highly variable in nature, and as interest rates decline, banks are given cover to cut how much they are paying depositors. But assets typically adjust lower at a slower pace. So the company has been a beneficiary of this, as well as changes to the composition of its balance sheet. Even so, picture would have been better had it not been for the fact that the provisions for credit losses for the institution grew considerably from $83.6 million to $165 million.

As net interest income improved, non-interest income increased also. It grew from $258.9 million in the first nine months of 2024 to $356.8 million the same time of 2025. There were multiple levers here, including a jump in wealth and an investment services fees revenue, an increase in service charges on deposit accounts, greater mortgage banking revenue, and a near doubling in capital markets income. A lot of this can be chalked up to the acquisition activities of the firm, though it's likely that there has been some organic growth here also. Regardless, this was instrumental in pushing net profits up to $440.6 million compared to the $373.2 million the institution reported the previous year.

If we project out results based on what we saw during the most recent quarter, the company would still be trading at a price to earnings multiple of 12.5. That is, unfortunately, higher than the 10 maximum that I typically like to see. Furthermore, it puts the institution around the middle of the group of the five banks that I compared it to in the chart above. Two of the five firms ended up being cheaper than it is, while the others are more expensive.

When it comes to the price to book and price to tangible book multiples, meanwhile, the picture looks less positive. Although the price to book multiple is not particularly high, relative to these same five institutions, it certainly is. Four of them are cheaper than it is on this basis, and they are also cheaper than it is on a price to tangible book basis. This can be seen in the chart below.

Normally, I'm open to being bullish about expensive companies if quality justifies it. However, Old National Bancorp isn't there quite yet. As the first chart below illustrates, the company does have a return on assets reading of 1.03%. That is above the 1% minimum I typically look for. But it's not drastically higher. And in that chart, you can see that two of the five banks that I compared it to are better off than it is in this regard. In the chart below that, I did the same type of comparative analysis using return on equity. In this case, the ranking relative to other similar firms is unchanged, but the 9.01% reading that we get is lower than the 10% maximum I prefer.

Another issue that I have with Old National Bancorp right now is credit quality. I typically like to see that no more than 0.50% of loans and assets are non-performing.

But in the image below, you can see that, as of the end of the most recent quarter, these numbers were 1.25% and 0.84% respectively. This is absolutely something that investors should continue to monitor, though I don't think it is worrisome enough that, if it stays unchanged, would warrant a bearish outlook.

At the start of this article, I mentioned that management will soon be announcing financial results for the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. This is expected to be on January 21st. Leading up to that point, analysts seem optimistic, with revenue forecasted to come in at $706.2 million. That would represent a big increase over the $489.9 million reported the previous year, which is calculated by taking the net interest income of the institution before factoring in its provision for credit losses, and adding it to non-interest income. Profitability is also forecasted to rise. The increase there would be from a per share earnings for the institution of $0.47 to $0.53. That would mean a jump in net profits from $149.8 million last year to $207 million this year. But even if we annualize that, it would still put the company's price to earnings multiple at about 10.8, which is still a bit above what I prefer to see.

Takeaway

Based on all the data provided, it is my belief that Old National Bancorp is essentially where it deserves to be. The company is certainly not undervalued, even under the best of assumptions. Perhaps further integration with Bremer will capture additional upside. Analysts certainly seem to think so based on projections for the final quarter of 2025. But until we see some reason to believe that being the case at a level that is in excess of what analysts are projecting, I maintain my Hold rating on the stock.

data.title: Align Technology Q4 Preview: Even After A Big Drop In Price, It's Too Expensive
data.description:
Introduction

It’s been around three years since the first time I covered Align Technology, Inc. (ALGN). At that time, I gave the company a Strong Sell rating, and said to brace for disappointment. In those 1091 days since that article, the SPDR® S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY) advanced around 74%, while ALGN dropped over 30%.

I wanted to revisit the company to see what has changed and go over what to expect in the next quarter, and what I will be looking out for. Given that its share price declined so much, while earnings continued to improve, the multiples contracted, but not enough for me to start a position. It is enough to upgrade it to a Hold rating for now and see how the demand for the product looks going into 2026.

Briefly on performance

Over the last three years, the company’s growth in sales has not been particularly impressive, especially in 2025. It seems that sales have plateaued and started to decline somewhat. The performance can be attributed to FX headwinds and pricing pressures, while volumes were more resilient.

Throughout 2025, the company’s main revenue generator saw lackluster performance while its other revenue segment was there to pick up the slack slightly. Even that one in the latest quarter showed revenue declines. So, it is not looking good regardless.

In terms of profitability and efficiency, we can see very little improvement in 3 years. 2025 numbers for gross margins are worse than they were back in 2023, most likely due to those pricing pressures I mentioned earlier. Nevertheless, the company maintains pretty decent margins, but it is worth keeping an eye out for how they continue to progress in 2026.

Other key efficiency and profitability metrics look to follow a similar pattern. They all peaked in 2024, and with the weakness in sales, we can see sub-10% returns all around. Will have to keep monitoring how these turn out in the near future.

Taking a look at the company’s financial position, ALGN finished the quarter with around $1B in cash and equivalents, against no long-term debt, so this is a good position to be in, especially when operationally, the company seems to be rather stagnant this year. The $1B can be used to further the growth of the company through higher spending on R&D in hopes that it will translate to higher sales in the future.

Overall, I see why the company’s shares have underperformed since the first time I covered it back in 2023. There is hardly any growth in either the top line or margins across the board. ALGN was trading at an almost 90 P/E ratio back in 2023. For some reason, investors were expecting miracles from the company, most likely due to the meme craze back then, but since that has cooled off, the company’s sky-high multiples contracted as its growth wavered. ALGN couldn’t keep up with such valuations.

What to Expect from Q4

Analysts are expecting the company to make $2.96 and $1.83 in adjusted and GAAP EPS, respectively, on $1.03B in sales. This translates to around 3.5% y/y and sequential growth, with full-year consensus at around $4B. So, more of the same is what I gather. There is still a lack of top-line growth to close off the fiscal year. How do these stack up against what the management is guiding? The numbers are more or less in line with the consensus. Additionally, the management expects to see Clear Aligner volume and ASP to be up sequentially, which is a good sign and a sign of some sort of turnaround. The management also guided for the non-GAAP operating margin to be around 26%, non-GAAP gross margins at around 71%, due to lower restructuring and other charges in Q4. For the full year, the non-GAAP operating margins will be slightly above 22.5%, which is in line with consensus estimates as well.

How likely is the company to beat or miss these estimates? Over the last ten quarters, the results were mixed, especially when we look at the sales expectations. The company missed half of the quarters, while missing 3 out of 10 on EPS.

I think since the expectations are in line all across the board, I would say the company should at least meet expectations in the upcoming quarter and wouldn’t be surprised to see a little beat on the top and bottom lines, but I don’t expect any extraordinary results.

What I’m Looking for

I will be looking out for an increase in cases for Q4 (due February 4). Evercore ISI notes that the website traffic to ALGN would suggest that Q4 case shipments will exceed the Street’s expectations of 651k and will be closer to 668k. This could mean that the company may well beat expectations by a decent margin in Q4, which will boost its top and bottom lines, especially if ASPs increase. In Q3, ASP stood at around $1,244, so if the management expects some sort of a recovery here, which according to the sales guidance, will be around 2%-4% recovery. Anything above that will be seen as very bullish and may push the company’s share price up quite a bit. In Q2, the company’s shares dropped around 35% on the day due to earnings not being what anyone expected, like weak sales, margin contraction, and a major restructuring to combat the said demand weakness, which cost the company around $160m in one-time charges, mostly non-cash.

I will be looking out for the progress on this restructuring and whether they are near completion, as the management said in the Q3 earnings call. I will be interested to see what kind of improvements this strategy is going to yield. The management expects to see 100bps-200bps improvements in margins for FY26 and beyond, so it will be interesting to see what that looks like.

In terms of margins, I will be looking out for any rebound going forward and whether the management expects this to be sustainable.

Is ALGN a Buy before Q4 earnings?

The easiest way I can think of to see whether the company is worth it is by running a valuation model. Below are the assumptions I used.

There is currently nothing to suggest that the company’s sales are going to shift into higher gear and accelerate from now on, so what I have here is good enough. For operating margins, I decided to take the company’s adjusted ones, as that is what everyone is focusing on, which takes out all the noise that may not necessarily reflect the company’s financial situation. I am assuming the company’s adjusted operating margins will continue to improve. The tax rate is set to be around 21%, as that is what the management expects its long-term charge to be. The rest I kept at its historical averages, as they look fine. With the assumptions above, we get the following EPS projections. These are more or less in line with analysts’ expectations.

With these projections, and a discount rate of 10% instead of the company’s WACC of 11.5%, which I thought was too conservative, especially now that the company’s share dropped considerably since its highs, we get the following fair value:

So, it looks like, even if we are being on the more optimistic side of things, the company is overvalued, but not by that much anymore, since all of the multiples contracted quite a bit since the crazy valuations back in 2021 and even 2023 when I covered it last. Nevertheless, the growth in sales and profitability doesn’t justify such a valuation. There is just no growth right now, but given it dropped over 69% in the last 5 years, I am going to upgrade it to a Hold and see how it progresses over the next year.

data.title: Conagra Brands: Not Out Of The Woods Just Yet -- Betting On AI To Sharpen Performance
data.description:
Intro

In my view, any investor looking at a food‑and‑beverage company should start by evaluating how well that business aligns with the underlying shifts in consumer eating habits. As I pointed out in my earlier piece on Conagra Brands, Inc. (CAG), back in November, the company sits in a fairly balanced position — parts of the portfolio face headwinds from these habit shifts, while other parts, particularly those in the chicken and protein-focused categories, enjoy solid tailwinds.

Lourival Monaco of Purdue University clearly explains how GLP‑1/weight‑loss drugs change food consumption:

The concept of healthy eating is not new, but with the introduction of drugs such as GLP-1s, the effects of lifestyle and diet changes are reaching a new level. Moreover, the rate at which these changes are occurring, driven by the effectiveness of these drugs, is unprecedented.

Another factor to watch is food inflation, which has pushed consumers toward cheaper options, including grocery private‑label products. That said, budget‑constrained customers are also cooking at home more and eating out less, which again creates a somewhat balanced setup for Conagra.

So overall, for Conagra, external forces are relatively neutral as I see it. That shifts the focus more toward execution — essentially, how well management can squeeze value out of every dollar invested in the business and adapt to changes in consumer behavior.

Again, Lourival Monaco of Purdue University:

These companies will likely need to redirect resources to produce more nutrient-dense, calorie-light options. This change could involve a major realignment in R&D, marketing, logistics, and procurement, with a decrease in focus on carbohydrates and an increase in focus on proteins.

Let’s take a closer look at changes in consumer food choices

The Purdue University article, based on a study from the Cornell SC Johnson College of Business have some more valuable insights:

One of the side effects of GLP-1 drugs is reduced food consumption, primarily due to appetite suppression and nausea. Consequently, this leads to lower food purchases among individuals taking these medications.

Households with at least one GLP-1 user reduced grocery spending by approximately 5.5% within six months of adoption. In financial terms, with an average monthly grocery spend of about $630, this 5.5% decline corresponds to a $416 reduction in annual food purchases for each household. Higher-income households, earning over $125,000, cut their grocery spending by 8.6%, equating to an annualized decrease of $690 – more than double the 4.2% decline ($270 annually) observed in households earning less than $125,000.

..the reduction in food purchases is not uniform across all food categories. The most significant declines occur in ultra-processed, calorie-dense items like chips, savory snacks, sweet bakery goods, sides, and cookies, with reductions ranging from -11.1% to -6.7%.

Pretty significant numbers, right? Roland Berger provides another insight that pulls these figures together:

With the expected growing number of GLP-1 users, this could have a potential impact of USD 60-90 bn on grocery spending in the US in 2031. The most affected product categories include chips & savory snacks (-11%), sweet bakery items (-9%) and soft drinks (-7%), which translates into short-to-medium term losses of USD 7.5-8 bn within these segments in the US market.

It seems to me that many food companies — especially the largest ones — can be a bit slow to accept and adapt to rapid, major changes in the marketplace. As is often the case, leaders in any industry tend to stay in their comfort zone when things are going reasonably well. In Conagra’s case, though, even as a large‑scale company that’s harder to turn than smaller peers, I think they’ve acknowledged the need for change clearly and consistently.

I paid close attention to management’s comments on this during their latest earnings‑call discussion.

Conagra is responding to changing market dynamics

Nielsen, a leading research firm, highlights a playbook for food companies based on their Food and Drink Trends for 2026:

The Four Macro Drivers Behind Every Bite

There are four key pillars underpinning these trends and characterizing consumer choice:

Quality – Quality-led consumers are on an upward trajectory, as high-quality ingredients (46%) and the quality of service (53%) emerge as key factors when they’re choosing where to eat out.

Experience – Consumers are drawn to venues offering a more immersive experience, (over-indexing among foodies and 18–34-year-olds).

Brand – On Premise visitors largely identify as brand-led, favoring trusted and innovative names.

Value – Less motivated by price, and more influenced by quality, provenance, and nutrition.

Some highlights from Conagra management’s comments on their portfolio and how they are aligning with underlying trends:

..right now, health and wellness is heavily, as everybody knows and can see, heavily about protein.

Young consumers, which we over-index with, are more focused on health and wellness than I've seen in a while, and it is playing right into some of our tailwind businesses like our protein-focused brands.

We have exactly the right snacks business for today’s consumers. Our portfolio of protein centric, high-fiber foods is in demand and on-trend. In an environment where some of our competitors are struggling with snack portfolios weighted toward salty and sugary carb-heavy products, our snacks are resonating strongly with consumers. This is a strategic advantage that positions us well for sustained growth.

..clean label continues to be really important as well as vegetable nutrition. So if you think about our portfolio with brands like Birds Eye Vegetables, which are just awesome vegetables flash frozen at the peak of rightness, you think about protein meat sticks as well as seeds and you think about our frozen businesses like Healthy Choice, which are incredibly clean label, incredibly healthy, high in protein, low in sugar, low in carbs, things like that, it's very well positioned. And so I probably feel like our portfolio is as well positioned today as it's been to compete in a world that's very focused on health and wellness.

In my view, management does a good job of explaining how they’re successfully adapting — and how those actions are showing up across the portfolio.

Their focus is understandably on the positives, with less attention on the segments that are still underperforming, since the reality is that organic growth remains down 3%. I think it’s essential for them to highlight the company’s strengths and reinforce confidence in their strategy. Hopefully, some of their actions will begin to show up in the numbers in the coming quarters.

Leveraging AI to improve operational efficiency

While it’s not a brand‑new initiative — it was already discussed in the earnings release — Seeking Alpha recently highlighted that "Conagra expects AI to unlock significant value across the company." According to SA, Conagra has highlighted partnerships and platforms such as Microsoft's (MSFT) Azure, OpenAI and Power Platform as part of its broader AI stack.

CEO Sean Connolly went on to discuss the topic further during the earnings release:

Now with technology kind of being democratized for even industries with our margin structure, the access to technologies to automate a lot of these business processes is kind of in an unprecedented place. And that's a pathway to more effectiveness and more efficiency going forward.

it's basically a reengineering of core business processes using especially AI for more effectiveness and more efficiency.

The AI initiative called Project Catalyst, further summarized by the CEO:

Project Catalyst will see a "fully dedicated team led by some of my most senior leaders" focused on reengineering core business processes using AI and automation, aiming for "more effectiveness and more efficiency going forward.

Q2 Earnings

Conagra’s fiscal Q2 revenue came in largely in line with expectations, while adjusted EPS beat by one cent at $0.45. Revenue actually dropped by 6.8%, but excluding the impact of divestitures, comparable organic sales were down by 3%.

Overall, all metrics saw noticeable YOY declines:

The sales breakdown highlights Refrigerated & Frozen as the weakest segment, though the CEO effectively downplayed it:

I wouldn't pay too much attention to Q2 year-on-year in frozen, because we had a blockbuster frozen quarter in Q2 last year. And our goal this quarter in frozen was to reclaim the market share that we basically loaned out to a competitor when we had supply constraints beginning last winter. And as you can see in the market share charts today, we've clawed back almost all of that. And our biggest business, frozen single-serve meals, we're almost up to 53%, which is pretty much the high watermark for us there.

An explanation for the significant decline in Q2 adjusted operating margin was presented:

It clearly shows how much production cost inflation hurts the bottom line — production costs were up 4.7%, making it the largest driver of margin decline, though this was partially offset by 1.4% in productivity gains.

Outlook

Since the company doesn’t provide quarterly outlooks, they just maintained their FY2026 guidance:

The CFO shared some extra notes regarding the outlook:

Capital allocation and dividend safety

There wasn’t any discussion about dividends during the latest earnings call, but in addition to the previously announced pause on share buybacks last quarter, the CFO reiterated the other details shared in the prior quarter’s conference call.

Free cash flow improved, moving from negative last quarter to positive this quarter. However, H1 FY26 FCF of $113 million still falls short of the $335 million dividend payout by $222 million.

This was balanced out by an M&A inflow of $649 million. The divestiture activity consisted of the Chef Boyardee brand sale, which generated net proceeds of $607.0 million, and also the sale of the frozen fish business—Van De Kamp’s and Mrs. Paul’s brands—for net proceeds of $41.9 million.

The proceeds also helped the company reduce its debt, but the weaker adjusted EBITDA still pushed the net leverage multiple up from 3.54 to 3.83:

With the company’s debt covenants capped at a net debt/EBITDA ratio of 4.5, there’s still plenty of breathing room to avoid any limits on dividend payouts in this regard.

In addition, the company has reached an agreement with a syndicate of financial institutions for a revolving credit facility with a maximum aggregate availability of $2.0 billion at any given time. The Amended Revolving Credit Agreement, which is unsecured, matures on June 27, 2030.

As the Company had not drawn on this facility as of the end of the latest quarter, ample liquidity remains available.

To sum up, I don’t see any immediate risk to the dividend at this time, but the company’s financial performance has quite a little room to deteriorate further.

Takeaway

Conagra management reaffirmed their confidence in delivering organic net sales growth in the second half of FY2026, even though the current quarter has not yet shown strong signs of improvement. Still, the relatively weak results were broadly anticipated and largely in line with market expectations. That said, the back half of the fiscal year will be an important test for the company.

Although the financial performance definitely needs improvement, the dividend appears safe for now.

The company’s new AI initiative, Project Catalyst, in my view, represents a meaningful step toward driving efficiency and supporting broader margin‑enhancement efforts.

To sum up, I think Conagra seems to be keeping up with changing consumer food trends, with a well-balanced portfolio that aligns with current market demands. While there’s still room for improvement, their progress is promising to deliver solid long-term returns for patient investors.
